course_description:
What drives criminal behaviour? This course introduces you to criminology's core questions: how we define, measure, and explain crime. You'll examine major theoretical perspectives, learn research methods, and develop the interpretive lenses needed to understand crime causation and criminal justice responses.

CRIM1000 provides an overview of the major approaches to understanding criminal behaviour, and how these approaches help us understand and respond to particular crime problems.

During this course we will focus on issues such as:

This course is designed to broadly survey how we study criminal behaviour, provide insights into different issues studied in criminology, and prepare you (by introducing foundational concepts) for more advanced study in criminology.

incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:

course_contacts.name: Associate Professor Suzanna Fay
course_contacts.notes: There are three ways that students can consult with the CRIM1000 Teaching Team 1. Attend office hours with your Course Coordinator during advertised times. See Blackboard for scheduled office hours. 2. Email your tutor directly via the messaging system on the CRIM1000 Blackboard page. 3. Attend a Scheduled Consultation Session. Before assessments are due we will run two face-to-face consultations sessions. Please see Blackboard for scheduled times and locations.

course_staff.name: Miss Rahma Waleed Nasir Mohamed Almazmi

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
In Semester 1 2026, CRIM1000 will be offered inﾠinternal mode only.ﾠThis is a first-year course and as such, engagement with students and course staff is critical for setting students up for success in this course and future university courses.
Students enrolled in CRIM1000 in Semester 1 2026ﾠwill have live face-to-faceﾠlectures and tutorialsﾠthat are timetabled on campus. Please ensure that you check the ECP course schedule and blackboard for when and where classes are held. If you miss a lecture, ECHO 360 recordings and lecture slides will be posted to our course Blackboard site to aid your ability to catch up on missed material.
Students are required to actively participate in tutorial workshops and lectures. There are multiple options for participation and these will be explained in the lecture duringﾠWeek 1.
Teaching staff do not have access to the timetabling system to help with class allocation. Therefore, should you need help with your timetable and/or allocation of classes, please ensure you email the School of Social Science Administration Team at
student.socsci@uq.edu.au
from your UQ student email account with the following details: full name, student ID, and course code.
course_aims: The aim of this course is to provide students with an overview of the nature of crime ﾠin Australian and international contexts.ﾠ It will provide a thorough grounding in the various criminological approaches to understanding the causes of criminal behaviour and will examine the major methods for measuring crime, as well as the dominant theoretical perspectives in the field of Criminology.

learning_outcomes.description: Explain foundational concepts and theories in criminology: Explain key criminological concepts, definitions, and major theoretical perspectives used to understand criminal behaviour.
Construct evidence-based criminological arguments: Develop clear, structured, and academically informed arguments supported by relevant research and data.
Find and synthesise criminological information: Locate, evaluate, and integrate information from diverse sources, including academic research, crime data, and media to inform criminological analysis.

Apply ethical approaches to criminological research and practice: Identify and critically assess ethical issues in criminological research and professional practice, including responsible use of AI and emerging technologies.
Demonstrate cultural competency in criminology: Recognise and appreciate how social diversity, inequality, and cultural contexts shape criminal behaviour and justice system responses.

assessment_summary.title: Tutorial Exercises
assessment_summary.due_date: Week 3 - Week 12; Tutorial Exercises are held in weeks 3 to 5, 7 to 9, and 11-12.

assessment_details.title: Crime Fact Checking Investigation
assessment_details.task_description: Due Date : Wednesday April 1st 2026, 2pm (Week 6) Weight : 20% (20 marks) Word count : 1500 words (+/- 10%) To complete Assessment 1 (Crime Fact Checking Investigation) and Assessment 3 (Theory Application Argument), you must choose a real crime incident. You can find information in the Week 1 folder AND in the Assessment folder on Blackboard Ultra on how to choose your crime incident. Why have I assigned this assessment: AI literacy is essential in modern criminology because artificial intelligence is increasingly used in policing, courts, and corrections—often with profound implications for justice and equity. Criminology students need to understand how AI systems can reproduce bias, how to verify AI-generated information, and how to evaluate algorithmic decision-making critically. This isn't just about using technology; it's about understanding how technology shapes crime, justice, and inequality. By developing AI literacy, students gain the analytical tools to work effectively in AI-mediated criminal justice systems while maintaining their commitment to fairness, accountability, and human dignity. PART 1: Select your crime case/incident Choose one crime news incident that has generated public discussion. This could be: · A specific criminal case focusing on an individual (e.g., a high-profile robbery, assault, fraud case) · A specific crime incident that involves more than one offender (E.g. 1999 WTO riot in Seattle, Cronulla race riot 2005) Requirements: · Must be from a credible news source (e.g., ABC News, The Guardian, BBC, local newspapers) · Include a link to your news article in your submission · May include international incidents Briefly describe the incident (100 words max) PART 2: AI-Generated Summaries Use at least 2 different AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, Copilot, Gemini) to generate summaries of your crime incident. Prompt each AI with: "Provide a detailed summary of [ crime incident name/description ] including: what happened, who was involved, the criminal charges or outcomes, and any key facts about the case." For each AI tool, document: · Which AI tool you used · The exact prompt you provided · Screenshot or copy of the AI's complete response · Brief comparison (50-100 words total): How did the two AI summaries differ? Which seemed more detailed or accurate at first glance? PART 3: Fact-Checking Claims Now become a fact-checker! Select the most detailed AI summary from Part A and verify its claims. Identify and fact-check at least 4 specific claims the AI made. For each claim: 1. The Claim (quote exactly what the AI said) 2. Fact-Check Result: · ✓ TRUE - Accurate · ⚠️ PARTIALLY TRUE - Mostly accurate but missing nuance/context · ✗ FALSE - Incorrect or misleading · ? UNVERIFIABLE - Cannot confirm with available sources 3. Evidence (150-200 words per claim): · What sources did you use to verify? (provide links/citations) · What did you find? · If false/misleading, what's the accurate information? · Why might the AI have gotten this wrong? PART 4: Critical Reflection Write 200-250 words reflecting on: · AI Accuracy: How accurate was the AI overall? What types of errors did it make (factual mistakes, hallucinations, oversimplifications, bias)? · Implications for Research: What are the risks of using AI for criminological research without verification? How should students use AI responsibly? · Your Process: What fact-checking strategies worked best? What challenges did you face? Please note that reproductions of Ai output do not count towards the total word count. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub . Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You must submit your assessment electronically by the due time, on the due date.
Your assessment must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra. To submit your assessment electronically log in to Blackboard Ultra with your UQ username and password and navigate to the relevant submission link for that assessment item on Blackboard Ultra. No emailed submissions of assessments will be accepted.
By submitting your assessment through Turnitin, you confirm that all work is your own, except where properly referenced. Only submit assessments that contain your original work.
You are required to retain proof of submission of your assessment. Your Digital Receipt is available for download from your Assignment Dashboard. If you cannot see your submission and download your digital receipt within a few hours, your assessment has not been successfully submitted; please submit again.
If you are experiencing technical difficulties with Blackboard, please contact the ITS Support Team .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Please note that from Semester 2, 2025 the Assessment Procedure has changed. You must submit your application on or before the assessment due date and time.
Grounds of an Extension
A list of acceptable reasons for an extension and the evidence you must provide can be found here . Your request may be refused if you do not meet the acceptable reasons for an extension. All extension requests must be accompanied by documentation that supports the reason for the request. The student submitting the request is fully responsible for all supporting documentation that is provided with the request and should ensure all documents are authentic.
Students registered with Student Support and Wellbeing Services / SAP
In the School of Social Science, extensions granted on the basis of registration with Student Support and Wellbeing Services (SSWS) or a Student Access Plan (SAP) will be the lesser of 7 calendar days or the maximum extension allowable for the assessment task.
More information is provided in Part D of the Assessment Procedure.
Discretionary Extensions
You are eligible for a discretionary extension for a single assessment task per semester of study for a duration of 48 hours, with no documentation required. You can apply if the assessment task allows (see the course profile for details) and if it is your first extension request for that assessment.
You cannot receive an extension using both discretionary grounds and SAP grounds for the same assessment and must select one basis when making their initial request.
Maximum Extension Requests
You can request a maximum of 3 extensions for the same assessment task. If a third extension is necessary, you must submit an Assessment Management Plan in addition to your supporting documentation with your request. If after the third approved extension you submit the assessment late, late penalties will apply.
Late Extension Applications
Late applications must include evidence of the reasons for the late request, detailing why you were unable to apply for an extension by the due date and time. The School of Social Science will not accept personal statements.
Additional information
Extension requests are processed and managed by the School of Social Science Administration Team. Course Coordinators are unable to grant extensions directly.
Extensions in your final semester of study could delay your graduation to the next graduation period.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Assessments will not be accepted if it is more than one week (7 calendar days) late without prior approval.

assessment_details.title: Tutorial Exercises
assessment_details.due_date: Week 3 - Week 12 Tutorial Exercises are held in weeks 3 to 5, 7 to 9, and 11-12.
assessment_details.task_description: Due Date: To be submitted at the end of the associated tutorial Weighting: 40% Tutorials in CRIM1000 are designed to do two things: First, give you important information and direction in completing your assessments. Second, to help you practice and develop your theory application and argument skills learned in the lecture and reading material each week. During tutorials in weeks 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11, and 12, you will complete an interactive exercise. These exercises are based on analysing and responding to real-world crime scenarios presented through videos, case studies, artifacts, or news reports shown in class. You will work through these scenarios by applying criminological theories and concepts to explain criminal behaviour, identify contributing factors, and develop analytical responses. These exercises are designed to help you practice critical thinking skills, engage with real-world crime scenarios, and demonstrate your understanding of lecture content and reading material through practical application. Explicit instructions on how each exercise works will be provided at the beginning of each tutorial. Assessment Security Requirements: These are secure, identity-verified assessments with the following requirements: · In-person attendance mandatory - Exercises can only be completed during your assigned tutorial · No digital devices permitted - Computers, phones, tablets, or other digital devices cannot be used during the exercise – unless stated otherwise. · Paper-based responses only - You must complete responses on the paper materials provided by your tutor · No external materials - Textbooks, notes, or outside materials are not permitted during the exercise · No AI assistance - Artificial intelligence tools cannot be used to assist or complete these exercises · Translation - translation dictionaries are allowed only. Failure to abide by these conditions may result in an academic misconduct allegation. Grading Structure: There are 8 exercises offered over the course of the semester. You may attempt all of them; however, your best 5 exercise scores will contribute to 40% of your final grade. Each exercise is worth 8 points (total possible: 40 points toward final grade). Everything you need to complete each exercise will be provided to you in class. Since you have multiple opportunities to complete exercises throughout the semester, no make-ups, extensions, or alternative arrangements are available. This is not a formal examination, so deferred assessment options do not apply. Why does this assessment matter? These interactive exercises are designed to help you apply theoretical knowledge to real-world crime scenarios in a supervised setting. They are designed to ensure that you are meeting the learning outcomes of this course and develop skills including critical thinking, give you an opportunity to demonstrate what you have learned, and build your confidence in theory application before your major assessment. In addition, these exercises help you to practice your exam-style thinking in a low-stakes environment and ensure the academic integrity of your learning. Read full task description Hide full task description

Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
Since you have multiple opportunities to complete exercises throughout the semester, no make-ups, extensions, or alternative arrangements are available. This is not a formal examination, so deferred assessment options do not apply.

assessment_details.title: Theory Application Argument
assessment_details.task_description: Due Date: Monday June 8th 2pm (Exam Week 1) Word Count: 1200 words (+/- 10%) Weighting: 40% (40 marks) HURDLE REQUIREMENT: You must submit an AI Usage Statement (template provided in Blackboard). Even if you did not use AI to support your writing, you must still submit the AI usage statement. Failure to do so will result in a grade of 0 for this assessment. You will apply a criminological theory to explain the criminal behaviour in your chosen crime case (from the Crime Fact Checking Investigation assessment). Your submission includes TWO components: (1) a focused written argument explaining how your theory applies to the case, and (2) a visual pathway diagram mapping the theoretical explanation into the specific facts of the case. Why have I assigned this task? Criminologists use theory to explain why crime occurs and why people offend. This assessment asks you to apply a theoretical framework to understand and explain a real world crime incident or criminal behaviour. The written component develops your ability to effectively construct an evidence-based argument, while the visual diagram helps you see and communicate the causal pathways that the theory proposes. Together, these skills are essential for anyone pursuing advanced criminological study and/or a career in criminal justice. Task Description: Part A: Written Component (1200 words - 25 marks) Your written argument must answer the following question: How does [insert chosen theory here] explain why [insert chosen offender here] engaged in criminal behaviour? OR How does [insert chosen theory here] explain how [insert chosen incident here] occurred? Your written argument should include: - A theory chosen covered in the course and contained in the list provided on Blackboard - An explanation of the central principles or key elements of the theory - And ONE example from peer-reviewed literature on how the theory has been applied to similar cases - An argument as to how your chosen theory explains the criminal behaviour in your chosen crime case - ONE critique of your chosen theory that is supported by evidence by peer-reviewed literature This is not a formal essay so there is no need for introductions/conclusions. Just focus on your argument. PART B: Theory Pathway Diagram (15 marks) You will create a one A4 page visual diagram of the causal pathway you are arguing is occurring to produce the criminal behaviour/incident. Examples of how to do this will be provided in lecture and in tutorials. You may draw this pathway by hand (as long as it can be clearly read) or digitally create the diagram pathway. Your diagram must visually demonstrate HOW your case maps onto the causal pathway proposed by your chosen theory. You must include examples/evidence from your chosen crime case on the theoretical diagram. Source Requirements: A minimum of 3 scholarly, peer-reviewed references Reliable sources about your chosen case (i.e. news reports, court documents, official reports) Technical Requirements: You may use AI to SUPPORT your writing. Tutorials will include a discussion of what appropriate AI usage looks like for this assessment. This course prepares you to be able to have ethical and responsible conversations and develop practices about using AI. The AI statement is linked to this component of the class. Please note that you must submit an AI statement regardless of whether you have used AI. Failure to do so will result in a 0 grade for this assessment. The visual diagram, reference list, and AI statement are not included in the word count. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Introduction: What makes something criminal? Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05
How can we really understanding Crime? Measuring the unmeasurable Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
Tutorial 1: Introduction to assessments and tutorial program Learning outcomes: L01
Who commits crime? Patterns and Stereotypes Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05
Tutorial 2: Tutorial Exercise Learning outcomes: L04
Does location matter? Neighbourhoods and crime How are our communities organised to prevent or promote crime? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Tutorial 3: Tutorial Exercise Learning outcomes: L02, L04
How do we learn criminal behaviour? The role of our associations How is our behaviour influenced by others? Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05
Tutorial 4: Tutorial Exercise Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Can criminal behaviour be taught? Social learning and crime What are the mechanisms by which we learn criminal techniques? Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05
No Tutorials this week.
Does inequality drive crime? Social structure and deviance Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
Tutorial 5: Tutorial Exercise Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

How does stress lead to crime? Understanding social strain How does one's criminal activity changes over the course of one's life and how might life events influence the propensity to offend? Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05
Tutorial 6: Tutorial Exercise Learning outcomes: L01, L02
What prevents crime? The power of social control How does inequality in Australia influence crime? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Tutorial 7: Tutorial Exercise Learning outcomes: L01, L03
NO LECTURE DUE TO LABOUR DAY PUBLIC HOLIDAY

Tutorial 8: CONSULTATION ONLY Scheduled tutorial times will be used for adhoc consultation with your tutors about your assessments. there are no prepared activities and no tutorial exercises. Learning outcomes: L02
Do labels create criminals? The impact of social reactions Does how we are labeled make a difference to criminality? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
Tutorial 9: Tutorial Exercise Learning outcomes: L02, L05
Who makes the rules? Power, conflict and the criminal law Who gets to decide who and what is criminal? Learning outcomes: L02, L03
Tutorial 10: Tutorial Exercise Learning outcomes: L02, L05
Where can a criminology degree take you: Career paths and applications Course summary and conversations about the utility of criminological education and skills. Learning outcomes: L01, L05
